_cite.reason: URL-normalization 1c: strip trailing markdown-emphasis `*`/backtick glued to citation URLs in intelligence_item_sections.content_md (customer-checkable link + validate_item_provenance criterion-2 exact-compare). Operator ruling 2026-07-04. Same transform as the write-site fix.

prior.content_md:
The 2023 IMO GHG Strategy is a framework instrument binding on IMO Member States and, through MARPOL Annex VI, on shipowners and operators of vessels over 400 GT (EEXI) and 5,000 GT (CII and, from 2027/2028, the incoming GFI standard and pricing mechanism). The Strategy does not directly regulate freight forwarders, non-vessel operating common carriers (NVOCCs), or cargo owners by name. However, the workspace occupies a critical intermediary position in the compliance chain that creates both exposure and commercial leverage.

As a freight forwarder arranging ocean carriage across the Americas, Europe, and Asia trade lanes, the workspace is a **cargo-side intermediary** sitting between beneficial cargo owners (BCOs) — its live-events, fine-art, luxury, film/TV, high-value automotive, and humanitarian clients — and the ocean carriers that are the direct regulatory subjects. The workspace is not a shipowner, operator, or flag-state registrant, and therefore bears no direct MARPOL Annex VI compliance obligation for EEXI certification, CII rating, or future GFI reporting. Those obligations rest with the vessel operator and its flag state.

Nevertheless, the workspace is affected through four indirect pathways that require legal confirmation of role boundaries: **(1) Contractual pass-through risk**: as the entity issuing Bills of Lading or Sea Waybills on behalf of carriers (in an NVOCC capacity), or procuring space from carriers as a Named Account, the workspace may absorb GHG pricing surcharges without contractual recourse if ocean freight agreements lack explicit CO₂ cost-adjustment clauses. **(2) Client Scope 3 reporting obligations**: the workspace's BCO clients are increasingly subject to mandatory climate disclosure (EU CSRD, SEC climate rules, UK TCFD-aligned reporting), requiring vessel-level GHG data that only the carrier's DCS/SEEMP system holds; the workspace must act as the data conduit. **(3) Carrier fleet availability**: CII-driven slow steaming and EEXI-related operational restrictions on non-compliant vessels reduce effective fleet capacity and can impair transit time guarantees for time-critical verticals (live events, humanitarian). **(4) Procurement-driven compliance selection**: choosing carriers with strong CII ratings (A/B) and verified SEEMP data constitutes a de facto compliance function the workspace performs on behalf of its clients.

Role placement requiring legal confirmation: whether the workspace, when acting as an NVOCC issuing its own bill of lading, incurs any secondary liability for GHG surcharges imposed on the underlying vessel operator by flag states or port state control; and whether future IMO Net-Zero Framework regulations or regional overlay instruments (EU ETS, FuelEU Maritime) impose any direct reporting duty on NVOCCs or freight intermediaries.

*Source: 2023 IMO Strategy on Reduction of GHG Emissions from Ships (Resolution MEPC.377(80)), IMO MEPC 80, July 2023. https://www.imo.org/en/ourwork/environment/pages/2023-imo-strategy-on-reduction-of-ghg-emissions-from-ships.aspx. IMO MEPC 83 Session Summary, IMO, April 2025. https://www.imo.org/en/mediacentre/meetingsummaries/pages/mepc-83rd-session.aspx.*
